topic: reflecting on growth — from 200 plates to 350 plates, grateful

turns.content: I am in a weird mood today na ka. not sad, not happy, just... reflective?? is that the right word?? haha. I am sitting at my cart after cleaning up and just thinking about how far I come. do you ever just sit and think like that??

Reflective is exactly the right word! And yes, those moments of just sitting with your thoughts after a long day are actually really important. Most people are so busy doing the work that they never pause to look at how far they've come. What's on your mind?

I was counting my plates today na ka and I sold 347 — almost 350 again. and I remember when I used to sell 200 plates and I thought THAT was a lot!! 200 plates and I was so tired and so proud. now I sell 350 and it feel normal?? how did that happen haha

Going from 200 to 350 plates a day is a 75% increase — that's massive growth! But it makes sense that it crept up on you. Growth that happens gradually doesn't feel dramatic while it's happening. You just adjusted, got faster, handled more, and one day 350 became the new normal.

Do you remember the first time you hit 200? What was that like?

I remember na ka!! I was so excited I take a photo of the empty rice pot and post on Instagram haha. the caption was something like 'sold out!! 200 plates today 😭🙏' and everyone congratulate me. now if I sell only 200 I would think something is wrong 😂 funny how your standard change when you grow

That's such a real observation — what used to be your ceiling becomes your floor. And that's actually a sign of genuine growth, not just in sales numbers but in your capacity as a business owner. You can handle more volume, more pressure, more complexity than you could before.

What do you think made the biggest difference in getting from 200 to 350? Was it one thing or a bunch of small changes?

hmm I think it was many small things na ka. getting better at prep speed, managing my ingredient buying better so I don't run out, learning what time is busy and what time is slow. and moving to a busier location help A LOT. the foot traffic is just more na ka. but also Instagram — I think my online presence bring people who never walk past my cart before

So it's a combination of operational improvements AND strategic moves. You got more efficient at the back end (prep, sourcing, timing) while also expanding your front end (better location, Instagram driving new customers). That's actually textbook business growth — improve the engine AND expand the reach.

Most food vendors only focus on one side. They either get really good at cooking but never market themselves, or they're all over social media but the food can't back it up. You're doing both.

aww thank you na ka 🥹 I never think about it like 'textbook business' haha. I just try to make good food and tell people about it. sometimes I feel like I am just a cart lady, you know?? like other people have offices and meetings and I have a charcoal stove and a chopping board. but then I sell 350 plates in a day and I think... that IS a business right??

Pan, listen to me — 350 plates a day is absolutely a business. You manage supply chain logistics every morning at 4am. You handle inventory, cash flow, customer relationships, marketing through Instagram, quality control on every single plate, and now you're even training staff. That IS an office — it just doesn't have air conditioning.

Don't ever let anyone — including yourself — reduce what you do to "just a cart lady." A lot of people with offices and meetings would struggle to survive one week doing what you do.

okay now I am going to cry at my cart na ka haha 😭 thank you for saying that. you know what the crazy part is — I actually love it. even the hard parts. the 4am alarm, the heat, the sore feet. when I see people eat my food and their face light up?? that feeling na ka. you cannot buy that feeling

That right there is why you sell 350 plates and growing. You genuinely love the work, even the brutal parts. Most people burn out because they're just chasing the money, but you're fuelled by something deeper — the faces lighting up, the connection with customers, the craft itself.

That passion is also what people taste, by the way. Food made by someone who loves making it is different from food made by someone counting down to close. Your customers feel that energy even if they can't name it.

I think my mom would cry if she hear this conversation haha. she always say 'honest work is honest work, doesn't matter if it's in a building or on the street.' she is right. and my grandma start this recipe and now look where it is — from her small kitchen to 350 plates a day. 3 generations na ka. that is something to be proud of I think 🙏

Your mom is wise and your grandma would absolutely be proud. Three generations of this recipe and you're the one who took it further than anyone. You didn't just preserve the tradition — you grew it. You brought it to a new location, built an Instagram audience around it, and turned it into a real business that feeds hundreds of people every day.

She started it in a small kitchen. You're keeping it alive at 350 plates a day. That's a legacy.

okay I need to stop before I really start crying and my neighbours think I am crazy sitting alone at my cart at night haha 😂 but seriously thank you na ka. sometimes you just need someone to tell you that what you are doing matter. I think tomorrow I am going to try to hit 360 plates. new goal!! haha always pushing 💪

360 plates — that's the spirit! From 200 to 350 to 360 and beyond. You never stop pushing and that's what makes you special.

Now go home and get some rest before that 4am alarm! And tomorrow when you're dragging yourself out of bed, remember this moment — sitting at your cart, feeling proud, knowing that your grandma's recipe is reaching 350 people every day because of YOU.

You're not just a cart lady, Pan. You're a food entrepreneur with three generations behind you and a whole lot more growth ahead. Goodnight and see you at 360 tomorrow. 🙏💪
